chloride 32 was treated with sodium azide in aqueous tetrahy-
drofuran to give acyl azide 33 , which was subjected to the ther-
mal decomposition. Despite heating in toluene in a sealed tube at
110 °C for 24 h, the acyl azide 33 was recovered without degra-
dation. Surprisingly, when 33 was heated in the presence of ( S )-
1-phenylethylamine ( 31 ) under the same reaction conditions, the
amide 35 was obtained as a sole product via nucleophilic acyl sub-
stitution. To confirm the structure of 35 as a chemical, a simi-
lar reaction of acyl chloride 32 with amine 34 led to product 35 .
Unfortunately, all efforts to synthesize the urea/thiourea 23 and
24 failed ( Scheme 7 ). After this attempted synthesis, we turned
our attention to synthesize their other analogs 39 and 40 . In this
context, amine 38 was prepared in three-step from commercially
